question: [File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IRatifier.isRatified / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER who is an authorized operator of the maker call EcrecoverRatifier.cancelRoot(maker, root) to cancel the Merkle root covering all of the maker's live offers, under PRECONDITIONS where the maker has previously called setIsAuthorized(attacker, true) for any reason, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: attacker calls cancelRoot(maker, root) -> any taker calls Midnight.take(offer, ratifierData, ...) -> EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified reverts with RootCanceled -> take reverts with RatifierFail, violating INVARIANT that a valid maker offer with unexpired expiry and unconsumed capacity must remain takeable, causing scoped impact: permanent DoS on all take flows for that maker's entire Merkle tree at zero cost beyond one cancelRoot call? Proof idea: fuzz (maker, root, authorizedAttacker) and assert that after cancelRoot, every take using any offer in that tree reverts regardless of units, taker, or ratifierData.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IRatifier.isRatified / SetterRatifier.setIsRootRatified / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER who is an authorized operator of the maker call SetterRatifier.setIsRootRatified(maker, root, false) to unratify a previously ratified root, under PRECONDITIONS where the maker authorized the attacker via setIsAuthorized and the SetterRatifier is the offer's ratifier, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: attacker calls setIsRootRatified(maker, root, false) -> taker calls Midnight.take(offer, ...) -> SetterRatifier.isRatified reverts with NotRatified -> take reverts with RatifierFail, violating INVARIANT that a maker's ratified offer must remain fillable until expiry or explicit cancellation by the maker, causing scoped impact: low-cost DoS on all take flows for that root? Proof idea: unit test asserting take reverts after setIsRootRatified(false) even with valid proof and unexpired offer.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: Midnight.take / isAuthorized] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER who is an authorized operator of the maker call Midnight.setIsAuthorized(ratifier, false, maker) to revoke the ratifier's authorization, under PRECONDITIONS where the maker had previously authorized the ratifier and the attacker has full coarse-grained authorization over the maker's state, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: attacker calls setIsAuthorized(ratifier, false, maker) -> any taker calls take(offer, ...) -> require(isAuthorized[offer.maker][offer.ratifier]) fails with RatifierUnauthorized, violating INVARIANT that a maker's published offer with valid ratifier must remain takeable, causing scoped impact: permanent DoS on all takes for that maker's offers at zero cost? Proof idea: fuzz (maker, ratifier, authorizedAttacker) and assert take reverts with RatifierUnauthorized after authorization revocation.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IRatifier.isRatified / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER supply a ratifierData bytes array of maximum feasible length (e.g., encoding a Merkle proof of height=20 with 20 proof elements) to cause out-of-gas in EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified or SetterRatifier.isRatified, under PRECONDITIONS where the offer uses a Merkle-based ratifier and the taker controls ratifierData, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: taker calls take(offer, largeRatifierData, ...) -> ratifier's isRatified iterates 20 Merkle proof steps in HashLib.isLeaf -> OOG revert -> take reverts, violating INVARIANT that a valid taker with correct ratifierData must be able to fill an offer, causing scoped impact: griefing DoS on take by forcing OOG with crafted proof length? Proof idea: fuzz proof.length from 0 to 20 and measure gas; assert that at height=20 the call still succeeds within block gas limit.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IRatifier.isRatified / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER deploy a malicious ratifier contract that always reverts in isRatified and convince a maker to authorize it, under PRECONDITIONS where the maker is tricked into calling setIsAuthorized(maliciousRatifier, true) and the offer specifies offer.ratifier = maliciousRatifier, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: taker calls take(offer, ...) -> IRatifier(maliciousRatifier).isRatified reverts -> take reverts with RatifierFail, violating INVARIANT that a maker's offer must be fillable by valid takers, causing scoped impact: permanent DoS on all takes for that offer? Proof idea: deploy a ratifier that always reverts; assert take always reverts regardless of ratifierData.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IRatifier.isRatified / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER deploy a ratifier that returns bytes32(0) instead of CALLBACK_SUCCESS and convince a maker to authorize it, under PRECONDITIONS where the maker authorizes the ratifier and the offer specifies it, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: taker calls take(offer, ...) -> IRatifier.isRatified returns bytes32(0) -> require(...== CALLBACK_SUCCESS) fails with RatifierFail -> take reverts, violating INVARIANT that a ratifier returning a non-CALLBACK_SUCCESS value must block take, causing scoped impact: DoS on all takes for that offer? Proof idea: unit test with IsRatifiedCallback.setReturnValue(bytes32(0)) confirming RatifierFail revert.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IRatifier.isRatified / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER deploy a ratifier that consumes all available gas in a tight loop during isRatified (declared as view but called via STATICCALL which still consumes gas), under PRECONDITIONS where the maker authorized this ratifier, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: taker calls take(offer, ...) with limited gas -> ratifier's isRatified burns all gas -> OOG propagates -> take reverts, violating INVARIANT that a take with sufficient gas for normal execution must succeed, causing scoped impact: griefing DoS on take by forcing callers to provide unbounded gas? Proof idea: deploy a gas-burning view ratifier; measure minimum gas required for take to succeed and assert it exceeds block gas limit.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER supply malformed ratifierData (e.g., empty bytes or incorrectly ABI-encoded data) to cause abi.decode to revert inside EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified, under PRECONDITIONS where the offer uses EcrecoverRatifier and the taker controls ratifierData, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: taker calls take(offer, malformedRatifierData, ...) -> abi.decode(ratifierData, (Signature, bytes32, uint256, bytes32[])) reverts -> take reverts with RatifierFail, violating INVARIANT that a taker with invalid ratifierData should revert cleanly without corrupting state, causing scoped impact: DoS on take for any taker who provides wrong ratifierData format? Proof idea: fuzz ratifierData with random bytes and assert take reverts without state changes.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER supply a ratifierData with a valid signature but wrong leafIndex, causing HashLib.isLeaf to return false and revert with InvalidProof, under PRECONDITIONS where the offer is in a multi-offer Merkle tree and the taker uses the wrong leafIndex, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: taker calls take(offer, ratifierDataWithWrongLeafIndex, ...) -> HashLib.isLeaf returns false -> require fails with InvalidProof -> take reverts, violating INVARIANT that a valid offer in a Merkle tree must be fillable with the correct proof, causing scoped impact: DoS on take if the correct leafIndex is not publicly known? Proof idea: fuzz leafIndex across [0, 2^proof.length) and assert only the correct index succeeds.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IRatifier.isRatified / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER who is an authorized operator of the maker call setIsAuthorized(ratifier, false, maker) and then setIsAuthorized(newMaliciousRatifier, true, maker) to replace the ratifier with a DoS ratifier, under PRECONDITIONS where the attacker has coarse-grained authorization over the maker, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: attacker replaces ratifier authorization -> all existing offers with old ratifier now fail RatifierUnauthorized -> new offers with malicious ratifier always revert, violating INVARIANT that a maker's published offers must remain fillable by valid takers, causing scoped impact: permanent DoS on all of the maker's take flows? Proof idea: state-transition test asserting that after authorization swap, all takes for old and new ratifier revert.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IRatifier.isRatified / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER observe that isRatified is called BEFORE consumed[offer.maker][offer.group] is updated (line 356 vs lines 367-373 in Midnight.sol), and deploy a ratifier that reads consumed state from Midnight to conditionally revert based on pre-take consumed value, under PRECONDITIONS where the ratifier is authorized and reads Midnight.consumed via STATICCALL-safe view, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: ratifier reads consumed[maker][group] -> if consumed == 0 revert, else pass -> first take always reverts, violating INVARIANT that a ratifier must not be able to selectively block the first fill of an offer, causing scoped impact: DoS on first take of any offer using this ratifier? Proof idea: deploy a ratifier that reads Midnight.consumed and reverts if consumed < threshold; assert first take reverts.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IRatifier.isRatified / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER deploy a ratifier that reads Midnight.liquidationLocked(id, seller) during isRatified and reverts if the seller is not locked, under PRECONDITIONS where the ratifier is authorized and the seller has no active take in progress, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: taker calls take(offer, ...) -> ratifier reads liquidationLocked -> seller not locked -> ratifier reverts -> take reverts, violating INVARIANT that a valid offer must be fillable when the seller is not in a callback, causing scoped impact: DoS on all normal takes for that offer? Proof idea: deploy a ratifier that checks liquidationLocked and reverts if false; assert take always reverts outside of nested take context.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IRatifier.isRatified / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER deploy a ratifier that reads block.timestamp and reverts if block.timestamp is within N seconds of offer.market.maturity, under PRECONDITIONS where the ratifier is authorized and the market is near maturity, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: time advances to maturity - N -> taker calls take(offer, ...) -> ratifier reads block.timestamp -> reverts -> take reverts, violating INVARIANT that a valid offer must be fillable at any time before expiry, causing scoped impact: DoS on take near maturity, preventing debt unwinding exactly when it is most needed? Proof idea: fuzz block.timestamp near maturity boundary and assert take reverts in the blocked window.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IRatifier.isRatified / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER deploy a ratifier that reads Midnight.debtOf(id, seller) and reverts if seller debt is above a threshold, under PRECONDITIONS where the ratifier is authorized and the seller has accumulated debt, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: seller accumulates debt -> taker calls take(offer, ...) -> ratifier reads debtOf -> debt > threshold -> ratifier reverts -> take reverts, violating INVARIANT that a valid offer must be fillable regardless of the seller's current debt level, causing scoped impact: DoS on take for over-leveraged sellers, preventing debt reduction via take? Proof idea: deploy a debt-checking ratifier; assert take reverts when seller debt exceeds threshold.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IRatifier.isRatified / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER deploy a ratifier that reads msg.sender (which is Midnight during isRatified) and cross-checks it against a stored expected address, and if the ratifier is deployed pointing to a different Midnight instance, always revert, under PRECONDITIONS where the ratifier's MIDNIGHT immutable is set to a different address than the actual Midnight, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: taker calls take(offer, ...) -> Midnight calls ratifier.isRatified -> ratifier checks msg.sender == MIDNIGHT -> mismatch -> reverts with NotMidnight -> take reverts, violating INVARIANT that a ratifier authorized by the maker must be callable by Midnight, causing scoped impact: DoS on take if ratifier is misconfigured? Proof idea: deploy EcrecoverRatifier with wrong MIDNIGHT address; assert take reverts with NotMidnight.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: EcrecoverRatifier.cancelRoot / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER who is an authorized operator of the maker front-run a pending take transaction by calling cancelRoot(maker, root) in the same block, under PRECONDITIONS where the attacker monitors the mempool and the maker has authorized the attacker, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: attacker observes pending take tx -> attacker submits cancelRoot(maker, root) with higher gas -> cancelRoot executes first -> take tx executes -> isRatified reverts with RootCanceled -> take reverts, violating INVARIANT that a valid take transaction must not be front-runnable by an authorized operator, causing scoped impact: low-cost front-running DoS on any take using EcrecoverRatifier? Proof idea: simulate mempool ordering; assert cancelRoot before take causes take to revert.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IRatifier.isRatified / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER deploy a ratifier that reads Midnight.marketState(id).lossFactor and reverts if lossFactor > 0, under PRECONDITIONS where the market has experienced bad debt and lossFactor is nonzero, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: bad debt is realized -> lossFactor increases -> taker calls take(offer, ...) -> ratifier reads lossFactor -> lossFactor > 0 -> ratifier reverts -> take reverts, violating INVARIANT that a valid offer must be fillable even after bad debt realization (as long as lossFactor < type(uint128).max), causing scoped impact: DoS on take in markets with any bad debt history? Proof idea: realize bad debt; deploy lossFactor-checking ratifier; assert take reverts.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IRatifier.isRatified / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER deploy a ratifier that reads Midnight.isAuthorized(offer.maker, taker) and reverts if the taker is not authorized by the maker, under PRECONDITIONS where the ratifier is authorized and the taker is not in the maker's authorization list, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: taker (not authorized by maker) calls take(offer, ...) -> ratifier reads isAuthorized(maker, taker) -> false -> ratifier reverts -> take reverts, violating INVARIANT that any address should be able to take a public offer without being authorized by the maker, causing scoped impact: DoS on take for all non-authorized takers? Proof idea: deploy an authorization-checking ratifier; assert take reverts for any taker not in maker's authorization list.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IRatifier.isRatified / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER deploy a ratifier that reads Midnight.creditOf(id, buyer) and reverts if the buyer already has credit, under PRECONDITIONS where the buyer (maker for buy offer) already has credit in the market, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: maker accumulates credit -> taker calls take(offer, ...) -> ratifier reads creditOf(id, maker) -> credit > 0 -> ratifier reverts -> take reverts, violating INVARIANT that a maker with existing credit must still be able to have their buy offer filled, causing scoped impact: DoS on take for makers with existing credit positions? Proof idea: establish maker credit; deploy credit-checking ratifier; assert take reverts.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IRatifier.isRatified / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER deploy a ratifier that reads Midnight.collateral(id, seller, index) and reverts if the seller's collateral is below a threshold, under PRECONDITIONS where the ratifier is authorized and the seller has low collateral, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: seller has low collateral -> taker calls take(offer, ...) -> ratifier reads collateral -> below threshold -> ratifier reverts -> take reverts, violating INVARIANT that a valid offer must be fillable regardless of the seller's collateral level (health is checked separately), causing scoped impact: DoS on take for undercollateralized sellers, preventing debt reduction? Proof idea: deploy collateral-checking ratifier; assert take reverts when seller collateral is low.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IRatifier.isRatified / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER who is an authorized operator of the maker call setConsumed(group, type(uint256).max, maker) to cancel all offers in the group, under PRECONDITIONS where the attacker has coarse-grained authorization over the maker, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: attacker calls setConsumed(group, type(uint256).max, maker) -> consumed[maker][group] = type(uint256).max -> taker calls take(offer, ...) -> consumed update overflows or exceeds maxUnits/maxAssets -> take reverts with ConsumedUnits/ConsumedAssets, violating INVARIANT that a maker's published offers must remain fillable until the maker explicitly cancels them, causing scoped impact: low-cost DoS on all takes for that group? Proof idea: assert that after setConsumed(type(uint256).max), all takes for that group revert.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IRatifier.isRatified / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER deploy a ratifier that reads Midnight.withdrawable(id) and reverts if withdrawable is zero, under PRECONDITIONS where the market has no withdrawable assets (no repayments yet), triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: market has zero withdrawable -> taker calls take(offer, ...) -> ratifier reads withdrawable -> zero -> ratifier reverts -> take reverts, violating INVARIANT that a valid offer must be fillable even in a market with no withdrawable assets, causing scoped impact: DoS on take in new markets before any repayments? Proof idea: deploy withdrawable-checking ratifier; assert take reverts in a fresh market.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER supply a ratifierData with a valid signature but a proof array where leafIndex >> proof.length != 0, causing HashLib.isLeaf to revert with LeafIndexOutOfRange, under PRECONDITIONS where the taker controls ratifierData and the offer uses EcrecoverRatifier, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: taker calls take(offer, ratifierDataWithOversizedLeafIndex, ...) -> HashLib.isLeaf reverts with LeafIndexOutOfRange -> take reverts, violating INVARIANT that invalid ratifierData must cause a clean revert without state changes, causing scoped impact: DoS on take for any taker who provides out-of-range leafIndex? Proof idea: fuzz leafIndex and proof.length; assert revert when leafIndex >> proof.length != 0.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: HashLib.offerTreeTypeHash / EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER supply a ratifierData with proof.length > 20, causing HashLib.offerTreeTypeHash to revert with TreeTooHigh, under PRECONDITIONS where the taker controls ratifierData and the offer uses EcrecoverRatifier, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: taker calls take(offer, ratifierDataWithProofLength21, ...) -> offerTreeTypeHash(21) reverts with TreeTooHigh -> take reverts, violating INVARIANT that a ratifier must handle all valid proof lengths without panicking, causing scoped impact: DoS on take for any taker who provides a proof with more than 20 elements? Proof idea: fuzz proof.length > 20 and assert TreeTooHigh revert.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: IRatifier.isRatified / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER deploy a ratifier that reads Midnight.claimableSettlementFee(loanToken) and reverts if it is above a threshold, under PRECONDITIONS where the market has accumulated settlement fees, triggering CALL_SEQUENCE: settlement fees accumulate -> taker calls take(offer, ...) -> ratifier reads claimableSettlementFee -> above threshold -> ratifier reverts -> take reverts, violating INVARIANT that a valid offer must be fillable regardless of accumulated protocol fees, causing scoped impact: DoS on take in high-volume markets? Proof idea: accumulate settlement fees; deploy fee-checking ratifier; assert take reverts.
